code Push the main boom done easy My airlock protocol holds every token under security Ooh a secure tokenizer Have you heard of the markdown files After every session how to handle memory for a long term Rocks all right so here check this out This'll be like my last story for the day It's very important very critical These large language models they do not like you to put too much context into the system If you use markdown files for memory you might be making a mistake because the quality of the output of the model falls off a cliff It gets things wrong or it does things weird if you use too much context So the ultimate goal is to have minimum context Do not use markdown files if possible If you do use markdown files and you need it to help the model understand something very special make sure it's one two or three lines in your markdown file and there's no more than that Do not use memory You don't want it to remember things You want it to be able to achieve a specific task They have been all optimized on goal achieving tasks So when you paste text like your prompt into the AI to do work for you make sure you give it a goal so it knows what its target is and you give it minimum possible information to be successful And make sure it's minimum Otherwise you're gonna spend more money burning tokens it's gonna go slower and you're gonna get a slower quality output So those are the tips for you today on the LLMs to generate code for you Thank you for the hearts you guys All right Avoid layers of abstraction Learn subtractive engineering principles Subtractive BERT used bidirectional encoder representation used an encoder decoder setup The encoder reads the entire input to create representations The decoder produces the outputs This made them great for specialized tasks like classifying or translation Latest models GPT series almost universally transitioned to the decoder only architecture By dropping the encoder the model acts purely as an with randomized positions of your data When you train it you don't want to Repetitively give it a whole bunch of data in a linear fashion because then it can't generalize as better This is a regularization approach to better generalize your model which is what you want when you're building AI You want it to be able to predict the correct data on data that it's never seen before So part of that you need to poke ways into the model to prevent it from overfitting Overfitting will allow the model to memorize something perfectly However it will not succeed as well when it sees data that it's never seen before It won't know what the correct answer is because it assumes that it knows something in a really good pattern It's like I've memorized the pattern perfectly Well there's your problem The world does not repeat patterns perfectly all the time right So you want to be able to generalize your model And part of the way to do that is to shuffle the data when you're Quick fix on Juniper here it is there we go okay the study is in discord link share wait what's the study they did a study oh the study right right right to understand yeah I want to see it let's check it out wait my Microsoft microwaves beeping it always is Brandon it always is okay wouldn't need to do that to learn for me all right let's take a look here oh all right let's take a look the more you know about AI the less you'll use it is that true let's think about that for a second how does that work here's your reminder that the more you know about AI the less you're likely to use it and a study just confirmed it across seven experiments with thousands of people worldwide these researchers gave these people a real objective test how well do you know AI and the test is difficult with questions about how the algorithm works the training data attention and the model behind it then they tracked who was actually using AI in their day to day activities what they found was that people who failed the test meaning people who didn't know much about AI they're using it every single day while people who aced that test they're barely touching it and to make sure this wasn't a fluke this was tested in multiple different places with college students everyday consumers they even pulled country level data across countries and the same patterns held up every single time the lower your AI knowledge the higher you're likely to use it they also walked into a major insurance company and asked senior executives who would you target to sell your new AI products and every single one of them said obviously go after the people who know and understand AI but that's not the case if you think about a magic show for example before you know how the trick works it's incredible you're amazed but the second someone explains that there's a trapdoor or you see the sleight of hand it becomes just a guy on stage that's fooling people that's kind of what's happening with the eye people who don't understand it watch it write some article or give some emotional advice and their brain sees it as this supernatural thing but people who actually understand AI see the exact same thing and think oh these here All right let's go start this up here Are LLMs conscious A new paper from Google DeepMind argues that no matter how good your prompts get LLMs will never be conscious Most people in AI believe in computational functionalism It's an idea that if you map the inputs and outputs of a brain and replicate them in code consciousness just emerges But researcher Alexander Lerchner argues that this is a fundamental mistake called abstraction fallacy The paper argues that computation isn't something that just exists in physics It's a map maker dependent description We as humans look at continuous voltages in a chip and alphabetize them into zeros and ones to give them meaning The AI isn't actually processing symbols It's just a physical substrate or silicon being manipulated by us to represent symbols Lerchner draws a hard line between simulation and instantiation Simulation is behavioral mimicry It's basically vibe coding a person Instantiation is the actual physical constitution that creates an existence The core argument is that algorithmic symbol manipulation the very things LLMs do is structurally incapable of creating experience And therefore it doesn't matter if you have trillion parameters or a perfect rack pipeline you are still just moving symbols around Think of it this way Consciousness isn't a software update that you can just install It's a physical reality of the hardware itself Basically they argue that you can't code your way into awareness anymore that you can code a calculator to actually feel the math it's doing In other words consciousness is a physical property not a mathematical one An LLM might pass the Turing test but it's still just a very complex calculator that feels nothing So basically we've built a perfect mirror of human intelligence but there's nobody actually behind the glass If you want to stay up to date with the latest deep tech research be sure to subscribe to the Better Stack channel Okay All right let me upload that real quick One second That was okay let's see what's going on there All right let me thumbs up that one All right so yes correct There's no consciousness It's just a machine right now scaling challenges because we have over a billion connected devices which is very very computational challenging right Also a lot of the operating systems had limits on number of concurrent connections and that created limitations on how many active connections we can have running to each Linux virtual machine So we had to there's a main problem So there's a five tuple problem So a five tuple problem that you could say You have so many inbound ports and outbound ports and the TCP layer You've got the source IP and the target IP and you've got the source port and the target port If the first two are the same you are at the limit of the connections on the last two of the five tuple And typically you can only reserve so many for inbound and outbound ports right That's a problem which means that you have a limit on the max number of IP address limit per box connection which is round it's like at the uppermost limit So we had to distribute our workload across many IP addresses for load balancing and things like that for that number of active number of connections It required a lot of innovation and a lot of thinking Additionally we had to do specific TCP kernel tuning settings in order to achieve that level of concurrency We had to increase memory buffers and max limits on file descriptors That's kind of common these days However back then it was novel So we were take a quick look at this short and then we're gonna head back into our code This is the Maya Rana Microsoft's new quantum chip and it is built on the strangest idea in all of computing that the way to stop a qubit from breaking is to hide it in two places at once The problem with creating a useful quantum computer is that right now qubits are catastrophically fragile Thermal noise or a single rogue electron can destroy the quantum of All right that was really funny If the electron is above the noise Microsoft are using a rare kind of architecture called a topological qubit When you call a superconducting semiconductor wire down to near Hey Sravana Zero the electrons pair up into Cooper pairs the bonded couples that carry current with zero resistance Sravana hi By applying a magnetic field it creates a distribution of the electron potential across that wire so they concentrate at the two ends a phenomenon called a Maya Rana zero mode By linking two of these wires together into an H shape called a tetron you produce a qubit where the electron information is spread across multiple locations and so it becomes much harder for the universe to break it Microsoft's Maya Rana one already showed improved parity lifetimes of around milliseconds from this architecture which is impressive considering most qubits today last around microseconds But there is still a failure mode The Cooper pairs in the qubit can be knocked apart by a stray photon or thermal fluctuation and can either enter or exit from the system Changing the overall qubit value from a zero to one or vice versa The energy cost of breaking one of these pairs is set by the superconductor's energy gap In the Maya Rana two the main improvement is switching out the aluminium superconductor from the Maya Rana one which had a gap of around microelectron volts and replacing it with lead which has a gap of about microelectron volts That improvement has increased the qubit lifetime from milliseconds to over seconds more than a times improvement The obvious question is why didn't they just use lead from the start And the answer to that is lead has to be deposited essentially atom by atom on the semiconductor at very low temperatures which it turns out is kind of hard to do Now if this holds up and that isn't to say there isn't skepticism from the physics community still around these announcements and how exactly these Maya Rana modes actually operate but if this system proves out Microsoft has now halved their own roadmap and now are suggesting that we could get to a scalable quantum computer as early as I got to speak to the lead scientist behind the project Dr Chetan Nayak and there is a full explainer dropping on YouTube soon If you like science and the idea that the safest place to hide something is everywhere all at once follow for more This is the Maya Rana Microsoft's new quantum chip and it is All right let's see here Hey Crafty good to see you Welcome on in
